one_liner: A 68-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis presents with 2 days of severe right knee pain, swelling, inability to bear weight, and a fever to 39.0 C.

stage2.hpi_extension: She has had chronic RA for 20 years, usually involving her hands and wrists. Two days ago she developed acute, severe pain and swelling of the right knee unlike her usual flares; the knee became hot and she could not walk on it. She has shaking chills. She denies recent trauma, tick exposure, sexual contact, or new sexual partners. No rash, urethritis, or conjunctivitis.
stage2.medications: Methotrexate weekly, etanercept (anti-TNF) weekly, prednisone 5 mg daily, levothyroxine, amlodipine
stage2.social_history: Retired librarian. Non-smoker. No IV drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: ill-appearing, febrile. Right knee: markedly swollen, warm, erythematous, and exquisitely tender with a large effusion and near-complete loss of active and passive range of motion due to pain. Unable to bear weight. No overlying ulcer or puncture wound. Left knee and hands show chronic RA deformities but no acute inflammation. No tenosynovitis, no pustular skin lesions, no genital discharge.

lab_results.result: Arthrocentesis of the right knee yields 45 mL of cloudy, purulent yellow fluid. Synovial fluid analysis: WBC 92,000/uL with 88% neutrophils (PMNs); glucose low; no crystals on polarized light microscopy. Gram stain: gram-positive cocci in clusters. Synovial fluid culture and blood cultures both grow Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Synovial fluid WBC >50,000 with >75% PMNs is the hallmark of septic arthritis. Staphylococcus aureus is the most common causative organism overall, especially in patients with RA or prosthetic joints. Positive blood cultures confirm bacteremic seeding.

lab_results.result: WBC 18.4 K/uL with 90% neutrophils and left shift. CRP 22 mg/dL. ESR 104 mm/hr. Lactate mildly elevated. Procalcitonin elevated.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Systemic inflammatory markers are markedly elevated and reinforce the diagnosis of a bacterial joint infection with bacteremia.

lab_results.result: Plain radiograph of the right knee shows a large joint effusion and chronic RA changes but no fracture, gas, or contiguous osteomyelitis.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Imaging confirms the effusion and excludes alternative diagnoses such as fracture or periarticular osteomyelitis but cannot distinguish infection from a sterile flare — arthrocentesis is required.

stage5.final_clue: She is taken emergently for arthroscopic joint washout and started on IV nafcillin. Pain improves within 48 hours and serial synovial fluid counts decline. She is counseled that any hot, painful monoarticular joint in a patient with RA must be tapped to exclude superimposed septic arthritis.

debrief.classic_presentation: Septic arthritis classically presents as an acutely hot, swollen, erythematous, and severely tender monoarticular joint with fever and severely limited range of motion. The knee is the most commonly affected joint in adults. Risk factors include rheumatoid arthritis, prosthetic joints, diabetes, immunosuppression, IV drug use, and bacteremia. In young sexually active patients, disseminated gonococcal infection produces a classic triad of tenosynovitis, dermatitis (pustular skin lesions), and polyarthritis/migratory arthralgias.
debrief.pathophysiology: Bacteria reach the joint most commonly via hematogenous seeding but also by direct inoculation or contiguous spread. Proliferation in synovial fluid triggers a neutrophil-rich inflammatory response with release of proteolytic enzymes that rapidly destroy articular cartilage within days. Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism across all age groups; Neisseria gonorrhoeae is the most common in young, sexually active adults; Streptococci and gram-negative rods (elderly, IVDU) also occur. Prosthetic joints are prone to infection with coagulase-negative staphylococci.
debrief.treatment_overview: Management is a true orthopedic emergency: urgent arthrocentesis for Gram stain, cell count, crystal analysis, and culture followed by joint drainage (arthroscopy, needle aspiration, or open washout) and empiric IV antibiotics covering S. aureus (vancomycin) pending cultures. Gonococcal arthritis is treated with ceftriaxone. Antibiotics are continued for 2-4 weeks with transition to oral therapy based on organism and clinical response.
high_yield_pearls: Synovial fluid WBC >50,000/uL with >75% PMNs is the classic finding in septic arthritis.
Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism overall; the knee is the most common joint.
In young sexually active adults, think disseminated gonococcal infection: tenosynovitis + dermatitis (pustular) + migratory polyarthritis triad.
Patients with rheumatoid arthritis or a prosthetic joint are at markedly increased risk — always aspirate a hot swollen joint to rule out infection.
Septic arthritis is an orthopedic emergency — delayed drainage leads to irreversible cartilage destruction within days.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Septic arthritis' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Arthritis — Synovial fluid analysis' section.
